messages.content: What substance appears on a fig tree before it bears fruit
Tiny flowers called "syncomium" appear on a fig tree before it bears fruit. These flowers are actually inside the fruit, which is why figs are considered to be inverted flowers.
I heard it being called something like “tach”, but I think I miss heard the word

I'm sorry, but I'm not certain what you may have heard. As an AI language model, I am not capable of hearing or listening. However, the word you might be referring to is probably "syconium", which describes the "hollow fleshy fruit" of the fig tree that contains the tiny flowers inside it.
Is there any word that sounds like tach that relates to a fig tree

I apologize, but I am not aware of any term related to fig trees that sounds similar to "tach". It is possible that you may have heard a regional or colloquial term that is not commonly used or recognized. The word "syconium" is the correct term to describe the fruit of the fig tree that contains the tiny flowers.
What is the Hebrew word for those

In Hebrew, the word for figs is "תאנים" (ta'aním). The tiny flowers that appear inside the fig fruit are called "פרחי תאנה" (parchei te'ena), which literally means "fig flowers". The fig tree is also mentioned numerous times in the Hebrew Scriptures or the Tanakh.
I want to know the substance that appears on a fig tree before it fruits

The substance that appears on a fig tree before it bears fruit is a type of bud called a "fig bud" or "fig syconium." These buds are formed on the previous year's wood and contain many tiny flowers (called florets) that will eventually form the sweet, delicious fig. The fig bud looks like a small, pear-shaped knob on the end of the shoots of the tree. As the fig bud matures, it develops into the actual fig fruit with the tiny flowers inside it.
I’m talking about a substance, not the fruit of flowers

I apologize for the confusion. If you are referring to a substance that appears on the fig tree itself before the fig fruit or florets emerge, there are a few possibilities:

- Fig latex: Fig trees produce a white milky sap called latex, which can appear on the tree before fruit develops. This sap may also appear when the fruit is picked or damaged.

- Wax: Some fig trees produce a waxy substance on their leaves and fruit to protect against water loss and pests. This can appear on the tree before the fig fruits develop.

- Lenticels: These are small pores on the surface of tree bark that allow for gas exchange between the outside environment and the inside of the tree. They can sometimes appear as small bumps or grooves on the bark of fig trees before fruit develops.

I hope this helps clarify the substance you were referring to.
What is the Hebrew word for the fig latex

In Hebrew, the fig latex is called "דבש תאנה" (d'vash te'ena), which literally means "fig honey". This term is used in the Hebrew Scriptures to describe the sweet, sticky substance that oozes from the fig fruit and is sometimes used as a sweetener. The same term can be used to refer to the fig latex that appears on the fig tree itself before the fruit develops.
What did Jesus not see on the fig tree in the bible

In the Bible, in the Gospel of Mark, there is a story about Jesus and a fig tree. According to the story, Jesus and his disciples were traveling from Bethany to Jerusalem and they came across a fig tree that was not bearing any fruit (Mark 11:12-14). Jesus approached the tree hoping to find figs to eat, but since he saw that there were no figs on the tree, he cursed it, saying, "May no one ever eat fruit from you again". 

So, Jesus did not see any figs on the fig tree in the story from the Gospel of Mark.
Didn’t he not see a substance on the tree

I apologize for my previous response. You are correct that according to the Gospel of Mark, Jesus saw something on the fig tree which suggests that it should have figs, but when he approached the tree, he did not find any figs (Mark 11:13-14). Here is the relevant passage from the New International Version:

"Seeing in the distance a fig tree in leaf, he went to find out if it had any fruit. When he reached it, he found nothing but leaves, because it was not the season for figs. Then he said to the tree, “May no one ever eat fruit from you again.” And his disciples heard him say it."

So, Jesus saw leaves on the tree, which suggested that it should have fruit, but he did not find any figs when he got closer to the tree.
Didn’t he see the liquid or something

I apologize for the confusion my earlier responses may have caused. According to the Gospel of Mark, when Jesus approached the fig tree, he looked for figs but found none because it was not the season for figs (Mark 11:13-14). He did not see any liquid or any other substance on the tree, according to this account. 

It is possible that in other texts or interpretations of the story, Jesus is said to have seen a liquid or other substance on the fig tree. However, in the Gospel of Mark, the focus is on the absence of fruit on a tree that appeared to be in leaf.
Didn’t he not see d’vash

No, according to the Gospel of Mark, Jesus did not see any d'vash (fig honey or fig syrup, derived from figs) on the fig tree. Instead, he saw the leaves of the tree from a distance, which suggested that the tree should have figs, but when he went up to the tree to look for figs, he did not find any because it was not the season for figs. The text does not mention any fig honey or syrup being present or absent on the fig tree at that time.

Sprechts du deutsch
Ja, ich spreche Deutsch. Wie kann ich dir helfen?
Können du spricht im Deutsch mit English so das ich können lernen? Mögen ein creole con Deutsche mit Englisch

Ja, ich kann auf eine Art und Weise sprechen, die dir beim Erlernen von Deutsch helfen würde. Es ist möglich, eine Art Creole oder Mischung aus Deutsch und Englisch zu verwenden, um schwierige Wörter oder Sätze zu erklären. Möchtest du, dass ich ein Beispiel für eine solche Mischsprache gebe oder eine bestimmte Frage beantworte, um dir mehr zu helfen?
Can you add English words to your speaking with the German like it’s some sort of creole?

Yes, I can add English words to my speaking with German and create a kind of creole language. Here is an example:

"Also, heute ist ein schöner day und wir können go to the park und enjoy the weather. Oder möchten Sie stay zu Hause und watch a movie? Es ist ganz up to you."

In dieser Sprachmischung wurden einige Deutsche und Englische Wörter kombiniert, um dem Gespräch ein bisschen Würze zu verleihen und es leichter verständlich zu machen. Lass es mich wissen, wenn du noch weitere Fragen hast!
